title: Tax Calculator — US Federal Income Tax 2024
description: Calculate your 2024 US federal income tax, effective tax rate, marginal rate, and take-home pay based on your filing status and income.
ymyl_disclaimer: This calculator provides estimates based on 2024 federal tax brackets. It does not account for state taxes, FICA, credits, or itemized deductions. Consult a tax professional for advice.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is the difference between effective tax rate and marginal tax rate?
faq.a1: The marginal tax rate is the rate applied to the last dollar of your taxable income — it is the highest bracket you reach. The effective tax rate is the average percentage paid across your entire income. The U.S. progressive tax system means only income above each bracket threshold is taxed at that bracket's rate — not your entire income. A single filer earning $80,000 in 2024 has a 22% marginal rate (their top bracket) but an effective rate of approximately 12.7%, because the first $11,600 is taxed at 10%, the next $35,550 at 12%, and only the remaining income at 22%. Effective rate is what you actually pay as a percentage of gross income.
faq.q2: What are the 2024 federal tax brackets for each filing status?
faq.a2: Single filers (taxable income): 10% on $0–$11,600; 12% on $11,601–$47,150; 22% on $47,151–$100,525; 24% on $100,526–$191,950; 32% on $191,951–$243,725; 35% on $243,726–$609,350; 37% above $609,350. Married Filing Jointly thresholds are approximately doubled. Head of Household thresholds fall between single and MFJ. Standard deductions: Single $14,600; MFJ $29,200; Head of Household $21,900. These deductions reduce gross income before applying the brackets.
faq.q3: Does this calculator include FICA taxes?
faq.a3: No — this calculator covers federal income tax only. FICA (Federal Insurance Contributions Act) consists of two separate taxes: Social Security at 6.2% on wages up to $168,600 (2024 wage base) and Medicare at 1.45% on all wages, plus a 0.9% additional Medicare tax for income above $200,000 (single) or $250,000 (MFJ). Together, FICA typically reduces take-home pay by an additional 7.65% compared to what this calculator shows. State income taxes (ranging from 0% in states like Texas and Florida to 13.3% in California) also reduce net pay further.
faq.q4: What is the standard deduction and should I itemize instead?
faq.a4: The standard deduction is a flat amount subtracted from gross income before tax brackets are applied — reducing your taxable income without requiring documentation. For 2024: $14,600 (Single), $29,200 (MFJ), $21,900 (Head of Household). Itemized deductions are specific expenses you can deduct instead: mortgage interest, state and local taxes (SALT, capped at $10,000), charitable contributions, and medical expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI. You should itemize only if your total itemized deductions exceed your applicable standard deduction — which, since the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 nearly doubled standard deductions, now applies to only about 12% of filers.
faq.q5: What does 'Head of Household' filing status mean?
faq.a5: Head of Household is a filing status for unmarried individuals who provide a home for a qualifying dependent — typically a child, but also certain relatives. To qualify, you must be unmarried (or considered unmarried) on December 31, have paid more than 50% of your home's costs during the year, and have a qualifying dependent who lived with you for more than half the year. HOH provides a larger standard deduction than Single ($21,900 vs. $14,600 in 2024) and more favorable bracket thresholds, recognizing the financial demands of single-parent households.
faq.q6: What is the alternative minimum tax (AMT)?
faq.a6: The Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) is a parallel tax system designed to ensure high-income filers pay a minimum level of tax regardless of deductions and credits. It was originally created to prevent very wealthy individuals from using legal deductions to eliminate their entire tax liability. The AMT exemption for 2024 is $85,700 for single filers and $133,300 for MFJ. Most middle-income earners are not subject to AMT, but those with very high deductions (large SALT deductions, ISO stock options, depreciation) may be. This calculator does not compute AMT — consult a tax professional if you have complex deductions.
faq.q7: How do tax credits differ from tax deductions?
faq.a7: Tax deductions reduce your taxable income, which saves you a percentage of the deduction equal to your marginal rate. A $1,000 deduction for someone in the 22% bracket saves $220 in tax. Tax credits directly reduce your tax liability dollar-for-dollar — a $1,000 credit saves $1,000 in tax regardless of your bracket. Common credits: Child Tax Credit ($2,000 per qualifying child under 17), Earned Income Tax Credit (up to $7,830 for families with 3+ children), Child and Dependent Care Credit, education credits (American Opportunity Credit up to $2,500, Lifetime Learning Credit up to $2,000). This calculator does not apply any credits — your actual tax liability may be significantly lower if you qualify for major credits.
faq.q8: Is it better to file jointly or separately if married?
faq.a8: Married Filing Jointly (MFJ) is nearly always better than Married Filing Separately (MFS) for most couples. MFJ provides a higher standard deduction ($29,200 vs. $14,600 each), more favorable bracket thresholds (approximately doubled vs. Single), access to the full Child Tax Credit, and eligibility for the Earned Income Tax Credit and education credits that are unavailable when filing separately. MFS may be advantageous in rare cases: when one spouse has very high medical expenses (the 7.5% AGI threshold applies to each spouse's income separately), when one spouse has income-based student loan repayment programs, or when spouses are legally separated and want independent tax liability. For most married couples, MFJ produces a lower combined tax bill.

content.whatIs:
The U.S. Federal Income Tax Calculator computes your 2024 estimated federal tax liability using the IRS's progressive bracket system, standard deduction, and your filing status. Enter your gross income and select Single, Married Filing Jointly, or Head of Household, and the calculator applies the step-by-step bracket calculation to produce your federal tax owed, effective tax rate, marginal rate, and estimated take-home pay.

The U.S. federal income tax system is progressive — meaning different portions of your income are taxed at different rates as you move up through seven brackets from 10% to 37%. This structure is frequently misunderstood: moving into a higher bracket does NOT mean all of your income is suddenly taxed at the higher rate. Only the income above each bracket threshold is taxed at the higher rate. The portion below remains taxed at the lower rate. A single filer earning $50,000 pays 10% on the first $11,600, 12% on the next $35,550, and 22% only on the remaining $2,850 — not 22% on the full $50,000.

This calculator applies the 2024 standard deduction automatically before running the bracket calculation. The standard deduction reduces taxable income without requiring any documentation or itemization — it is the simplest and most widely used method for reducing tax liability. Additional factors that may reduce your actual tax — state income taxes, FICA payroll taxes, tax credits, 401(k) pre-tax contributions, HSA contributions, and itemized deductions — are not modeled here and should be consulted with a tax professional for a complete picture.
content.formula:
Step 1 — Apply Standard Deduction:
Taxable Income = max(0, Gross Income − Standard Deduction)

Standard Deductions (2024):
  Single:               $14,600
  Married Filing Jointly: $29,200
  Head of Household:    $21,900

Step 2 — Apply 2024 Tax Brackets (Single example):
  10% on first $11,600 of taxable income
  12% on $11,601–$47,150
  22% on $47,151–$100,525
  24% on $100,526–$191,950
  32% on $191,951–$243,725
  35% on $243,726–$609,350
  37% above $609,350

Step 3 — Compute rates:
Federal Tax = sum of income in each bracket × bracket rate
Effective Rate = Federal Tax ÷ Gross Income × 100
Marginal Rate = rate of highest bracket reached
Take-Home = Gross Income − Federal Tax
(Note: FICA and state taxes are not included)
content.example:
Example 1 — Single filer, $75,000 gross:
Standard deduction: $14,600 → Taxable income: $60,400
Bracket math:
  10% × $11,600 = $1,160.00
  12% × $35,550 = $4,266.00
  22% × $13,250 = $2,915.00
Federal tax: $8,341. Effective rate: 11.1%. Marginal rate: 22%.
Estimated take-home (federal only): $66,659.

Example 2 — Married Filing Jointly, $150,000 gross:
Standard deduction: $29,200 → Taxable income: $120,800
Bracket math (MFJ brackets):
  10% × $23,200 = $2,320
  12% × $71,050 = $8,526
  22% × $26,550 = $5,841
Federal tax: $16,687. Effective rate: 11.1%. Marginal rate: 22%.

Example 3 — Head of Household, $55,000 gross:
Standard deduction: $21,900 → Taxable income: $33,100
Bracket math (HOH brackets):
  10% × $16,550 = $1,655
  12% × $16,550 = $1,986
Federal tax: $3,641. Effective rate: 6.6%. Marginal rate: 12%.
content.howItWorks_1: Enter your gross annual income — the total wages, salary, or self-employment income before any deductions or taxes. Select your filing status: Single (unmarried or legally separated), Married Filing Jointly (the most common and usually most beneficial for married couples), or Head of Household (unmarried with a qualifying dependent).
content.howItWorks_2: Click Calculate. The calculator first subtracts the 2024 standard deduction for your filing status to produce taxable income. It then applies the progressive bracket rates in sequence — taxing each income layer at its applicable rate — summing the results to produce your total federal tax liability.
content.howItWorks_3: Review all six outputs: Federal Tax (total owed), Effective Rate (what you actually pay as a percentage of gross income), Marginal Rate (your top bracket — relevant for evaluating deductions), Standard Deduction applied, Taxable Income after deduction, and Take-Home Pay after federal tax. For true net pay, subtract FICA (7.65%), state income tax, and any applicable local taxes from this take-home figure.
content.whyItMatters:
Understanding your federal income tax calculation is foundational to making effective financial decisions — from salary negotiation to retirement contributions to investment strategy. One of the most consequential and widely misunderstood tax concepts is the marginal rate versus effective rate distinction. Many employees who see they are in the 22% or 24% tax bracket assume they pay that percentage on all their income and are therefore reluctant to earn more, take overtime, or receive bonuses. This 'bracket fear' is mathematically unfounded — the 22% rate applies only to the income above the 22% threshold, not to everything earned below it.

Pre-tax retirement contributions are one of the most powerful tax strategies available to working Americans, and understanding your marginal rate makes their value concrete. A $1,000 contribution to a traditional 401(k) by someone in the 22% marginal bracket reduces their federal tax bill by $220 immediately — the government effectively subsidizes retirement savings by the employee's marginal rate. For someone in the 24% bracket, each $1,000 in 401(k) contribution saves $240 in federal taxes this year. Over a 30-year career, the compounded tax savings from pre-tax contributions can amount to hundreds of thousands of dollars in additional retirement wealth.

Tax planning is also relevant for major life events: selling a home, exercising stock options, receiving an inheritance, or starting a business all have federal tax implications that can be estimated in advance. Knowing your current effective and marginal rates allows you to model these scenarios — such as whether a Roth IRA conversion makes sense this year when your income is temporarily lower — and to time decisions accordingly. The marginal rate is also the correct rate to use when evaluating itemized deductions: a mortgage interest deduction of $15,000 saves you $15,000 × marginal rate in federal taxes.
content.limitations:
This calculator computes federal income tax only — it does not include FICA payroll taxes (Social Security 6.2% + Medicare 1.45% = 7.65% combined, paid on earned income), state income taxes (which range from 0% to 13.3%), local city taxes, or self-employment tax (15.3% for self-employed individuals, covering both employer and employee FICA contributions). For a complete take-home pay calculation, all of these must be subtracted from gross income separately.

The calculator applies only the standard deduction and does not model itemized deductions, above-the-line deductions (traditional IRA contributions, student loan interest, health savings account contributions, alimony paid under pre-2019 agreements), tax credits (Child Tax Credit, EITC, education credits, saver's credit), or the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT). These items can significantly reduce actual tax liability below the figure shown here. The IRS Free File tool or tax preparation software (TurboTax, H&R Block, TaxAct) applies all of these factors and is the appropriate tool for actual tax filing.

Tax law changes annually. While this calculator uses 2024 IRS figures (inflation-adjusted from 2023 under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act), bracket thresholds, standard deductions, and credit amounts are adjusted each year. If you are estimating taxes for a year other than 2024, the figures shown may not apply. Always verify current-year figures on IRS.gov or consult a CPA for tax year-specific calculations.
content.tips_1: Maximize pre-tax retirement contributions to reduce your taxable income dollar-for-dollar. In 2024, you can contribute up to $23,000 to a 401(k) ($30,500 if age 50+) and up to $7,000 to a traditional IRA ($8,000 if 50+). Each dollar contributed at your marginal rate reduces your tax bill by that percentage — a 22% bracket filer saves $5,060 in federal tax by maxing their 401(k) contribution alone, and defers all growth tax-free until withdrawal.
content.tips_2: Understand your effective rate (not just your marginal rate) when evaluating job offers, raises, or side income. A $10,000 raise for someone in the 22% bracket does not cost $2,200 in tax — it costs 22% of the marginal amount, but the effective rate on total income remains lower. The actual tax increase is roughly $2,200 in federal tax plus FICA on the incremental income, leaving approximately $7,100 in additional net pay — a meaningful improvement despite the tax.
content.tips_3: Consider tax-loss harvesting in taxable investment accounts before year-end. Realizing investment losses in December offsets capital gains dollar-for-dollar and can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income, reducing your federal tax bill. Losses beyond $3,000 carry forward to future tax years. This strategy is most valuable for investors in the 22%+ bracket who have had a mixed year of investment gains and losses.
content.tips_4: If you are self-employed, deduct the employer-equivalent half of self-employment tax (7.65%) as an above-the-line deduction on Schedule SE. Self-employed individuals pay 15.3% in self-employment tax but can deduct half (7.65%) from gross income before federal tax brackets apply. This deduction significantly reduces the net tax burden of self-employment relative to the raw 15.3% figure, particularly in higher income brackets.
